observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct: a. the MA and the Assistant Municipal Treasurer (MT) to (i) conduct periodic reconciliation of their records and take up the necessary adjusting entries in the books, as appropriate; (ii) exert diligent efforts to obtain from the bank the required documentation like bank debit and credit advice as bases in making the necessary adjustments in the books; and (iii) coordinate with the depository bank for possible correction/adjustment of the bank reconciling items amounting to ₱295,353.76 (net); and b. the MA to (i) correct balances of the CIB-LCCA and the corresponding payable and government equity accounts; and (ii) record promptly valid book reconciling items in the books of accounts, duly substantiated with appropriate documentation such as but not limited to, copies of debit and credit memos; and c) the Acting MT to record the identified reconciling items in the cashbook.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The Municipal Accountant (MA) and the Assistant MT failed anew to conduct periodic reconciliation. Copies of Notice of Authority to Debit Account Issued are maintained by the Treasury Office. JEVs were prepared. JEV Nos. 4 1/31/2024; JEV Nos. 68-70 all dated 8/31/2024) BRS were prepared monthly, however, valid reconciling items were not promptly adjusted.
observations.reason: Copies of Notice of Authority to Debit Account Issued are maintained by the Treasury Office. JEVs were prepared. BRS were prepared monthly, however, valid reconciling items were not promptly adjusted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to: a) instruct the MA to (i) in coordination with the Municipal Engineer (ME), record the cost of partially completed infrastructure projects at year-end under the CIP account and the corresponding Accounts Payable thereof; and (ii) draw the adjusting JEVs to correct the erroneous recording/non-recording of completed projects to their appropriate Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) accounts and corresponding accumulated depreciation; b) ensure the commitment of the MEO to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) a report supported with SWA regarding the status/progress of the construction of all ongoing projects monthly, especially at year-end to facilitate the timely recording of CIP in the books.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented JEVs were not prepared due to non- submission of the ME of Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA) at year-end.
observations.reason: MEO was not able to submit to the MAO a monitoring report supported with SWA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management instruct the MA to abide strictly with COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 08, 2015 to ensure a timely review of the transactions. Likewise, maintain an subsidiary ledger solely for CF and place the CA voucher with its supporting documents in a sealed envelope when submitting the same to COA Audit Team Leader for post-audit to protect the confidentiality of the transaction.
observations.managementAction: Not Promptly complied with. Submission of CA DVs for CF was not consistently complied with.
observations.reason: The CA DVs for CF were not consistently submitted within the required timelines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE to: a) instruct the MTO to undertake the following courses of action: (i) prepare a stratified schedule of presumptive income level every tax period to approximate the gross sales or receipt of each business classification and submit a representation to the Sangguniang Bayan to set up or institutionalize the use of the same; (ii) regularly examine the books of accounts of business establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales or receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid pursuant to Section 171 of R.A. No. 7160; (iii) assign personnel at the MTO who are well-equipped with the necessary knowledge and training in the examination of books of accounts to enable the said Office to carry on with their mandate efficiently; (iv) tag all the aforesaid suppliers and contractors with underdeclared sales or receipts and be subject to examination of books of accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees, and charges to be collected; (v) require the concerned business establishments to settle their unpaid business taxes prior to their closure/retirement to pay their taxes due based on the submitted Sworn Statement of Gross Sales or Receipts; (vi) require the concerned supplier with the closed business but continued to operate, to apply for renewal, pay the renewal fee and business tax, as well as other fees, penalties and charges, for the period identified; and impose possible sanctions for non-compliance with the RRC; (vii) coordinate with the ME to identify the contractors who have bunkhouses or other structures that may be considered as project office as a basis for the imposition of business tax; (viii) collect business taxes from outside contractors who engaged in business within the Municipality applying the situs of taxation; (ix) instruct the eBPLS personnel-in-charge of the Electronic Business Processing System (eBPLS) to custom-fit or adjust the system’s default setup with the provisions of its RRC to avoid inaccurate computation of surcharges and interests; and b) advise the designated BPLO to (i) update records of registered business establishment; and (ii) require the submission of complete requirements for the application or renewal of business permits as required under Section 3A.03 (b)(1) and (2) of the RRC, prior to the issuance of business permits; and c) require the MT and the Designated BPLO to devise with the Municipality measures to validate the declared gross sales or receipts of establishments applying for business permits especially those who are having direct transactions with the Municipality and its Barangays.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented Stratified schedule of presumptive income level was not prepared. Examination of books of accounts (EBA) of business establishment was not conducted. Records of registered business establishments were updated. Demand letters/Notices were sent. The collection of business taxes from outside contractors is not consistently done.
observations.reason: Personnel were not able to attend seminars involving examination of books of accounts (EBA). The collection of business taxes from outside contractors is not consistently done. Stratified schedule of presumptive income level was not prepared.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that the LCE instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and the ME: a) to abide strictly by the project schedule by conducting regular monitoring of the status of project implementation; and b) together with the Local Development Council (LDC) and end-users to properly plan the PPAs to be included in the 20% Local Development Plan, to ensure the full implementation of all the priority projects targeted for the year for the benefit of the Municipality and the constituents in general. We also recommended that the LCE instruct the MPDC and implementing units to: c) together with the LDC to prepare Comprehensive Multi-sectoral Development Plans (CDP), and Local Development Investment Program (LDIP) as required under Sections 106 and 109 of the Local Government Code and the Guidebook issued by DILG, respectively; d) plan intelligently, prepare detailed engineering and Program of Works (PoWs), and consider the needs of the constituents in formulating the developmental plans; and e) conduct prioritization of PPAs contained in the AIP so that specific dates of implementation could be determined for the proper implementation and monitoring of the same and avoid possible delays during the execution of projects. Finally, we recommended the BAC to require all winning bidders to submit their respective Construction Safety and Health Program (CSHP) to DOLE for approval before implementation of the project; and the ME to monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP of the contractors.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented Project schedule was not strictly abided. The Fund was not optimally utilized for CY 2024. Detailed engineering and Program of Works were prepared only upon implementation. The BAC required the winning bidders to submit their CHSP to DOLE for approval before implementation of the project; and the ME was to monitor implementation.
observations.reason: Project schedules not strictly followed; LDIP and CDP not prepared and finalized during the year; some PPAs not implementation-ready.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE, through the SWM Board, designated Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO), undertake the following courses of action: a) prioritize the implementation of solid waste management programs and consider increasing the budget to align/harmonize with SWMP, once approved by the NSWMC; b) fast-track the revision of the SWMP through compliance with the requirements in the annotated outline issued by the Department of Environment and Natural Resources-Environmental Management Bureau (DENR-EMB) III, for endorsement and approval of the NSWMC; c) stop the practice of collecting and dumping mixed wastes in the open/controlled dumpsite pursuant to the DENR Closure Notice to the LGU on February 2011; (ii) collect only residual wastes; (iii) consider availing services of accredited landfills for the disposal of residual municipal solid wastes; d) on the implementation of the Comprehensive ESWM ordinance: (i) exercise strong political will to implement the environmental laws and comply with the proper segregation, collection, and disposal of wastes; (ii) strictly implement the mandatory segregation of wastes at source thru conducting an extensive information education campaign; (iii) involve closely the LGU officials and business establishments in the strict implementation of the existing environmental law and intensify information campaign towards the “no segregation, no collection policy” among the residents; (iv) create an inspection unit that will impose the fines and penalties against violators of prohibited acts to improve compliance rate and provide sufficient funding for SWM PPAs; and (v) increase the compliance rate of constituents by educating the public through the conduct of continuous forums and seminars; e) hire additional SWM personnel in charge of the Municipal MRF and require the construction of MRF in every barangay or, if possible, resort to clustering of MRF in small barangays with limited resources to comply with the Act; and f) hire additional garbage collectors and ensure that waste collectors are equipped with PPEs to safeguard them from hazards of handling solid wastes; and g) expedite the purchase of vehicles specifically designed for the collection, transport, and disposal of solid wastes compliant with the aforementioned requirements.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The draft SWM Plan was submitted but is still subject for revisions. The LGU has not availed services of accredited landfills. The practice of collecting and dumping mixed wastes in the open/controlled dumpsite was not fully stopped. Additional SWM personnel were assigned in the charge of the MRF. Vehicles designed for collection and disposal of solid wastes were procured.
observations.reason: Draft SWM Plan still subject for revisions; services of accredited landfills not availed; open dumpsite practices not fully stopped.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Accounting Office (a) ensure that all contributions and loans withheld during the period will be remitted in full; (b) conduct monthly reconciliation of contributions and loans withheld and remittances with the accounting records and effect necessary adjustments for any discrepancies noted; and (c) trace the unverified balance of ₱203,668.41 with accounting records, payrolls, and remittances; and make representation with the GSIS for the necessary reconciliation of records. Moreover, draw the adjusting entry to correct the non-recording of employer’s share for the TLB of the former employees.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented Continued remittances were made but reconciliation is ongoing. Adjusting entries to correct non-recording of employer’s share for TLB were prepared (JEVs prepared).
observations.reason: Reconciliation is ongoing; adjusting entries will be made after due reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require all persons responsible to settle immediately the audit suspensions and disallowances of ₱94,022,139.18.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented No management action.

observations.recommendation:
1.16 We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive
(LCE) instruct:
a) the Municipal Accountant (MA) and the Acting MT to (i) obtain from the
bank the necessary documentation such as bank debit and credit advice, to
serve as the basis for adjustments in the books of accounts; and (ii) conduct
regular reconciliation of accounting ledgers and cashbooks, at least on a
quarterly basis, to promptly identify and resolve discrepancies, if any;
b) the MA to (i) correct balances of the CIB-LCCA and the corresponding
payable and government equity accounts; and (ii) record promptly valid
book reconciling items in the books of accounts, duly substantiated with
appropriate documentation such as but not limited to, copies of debit and
credit memos; and
c) the Acting MT to record the identified reconciling items in the cashbook.

1.17 The Acting MT commented that most of the bank reconciling items identified, such
as credit memoranda, represented the Municipality’s share in the Real Property Tax (RPT)
received from the Provincial Government. Moreover, Management acknowledged the
audit observations and committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

2.17 We commended Management for its compliance with regulations governing
the grant and utilization of CAs issued to SDOs. However, we recommended and
Management agreed to install stringent measures in the liquidation of the CAs, such
as, but not limited to (a) the grant of additional CAs only upon the liquidation of
previous CAs; (b) designation of other SDOs to accommodate some of the CAs and
prevent overlapping; and (c) ensure that no CA remains unliquidated as of year-end.

3.16 We recommended and Management agreed to undertake the following courses
of action:
a. instruct the Acting MT to furnish the MA of a duly Certified List of Taxpayers
and Taxes Due showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts due and
collectible for the year to serve as the basis of recording;
b. direct the MA to (i) make use of the Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due
to be submitted by the Acting MT as the basis for setting up the RPT/SET
Receivables and Deferred Credits at the beginning of the year; and (ii)
establish SLs to record receivables and subsequent collections to regularly
monitor outstanding balances of taxpayers. This will enhance the accuracy of
financial records and enable more effective tracking and management of
collectible accounts.
c. Finally, instruct the MA to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to
correct the misstatements in Receivables and Revenue accounts:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
RPT Receivable ₱20,286,176.95
SET Receivable 20,286,176.95
Deferred RPT 20,286,176.95
Deferred SET 20,286,176.95
To correct the understatement in Receivables and Deferred Income.

4.32 We recommended and Management agreed to the LCE instruct the:
a. MA to (i) derecognize inventory items in the books that have already been
consumed or distributed to end-users in timely manner; (ii) recognize in
the books value of drugs and medicines received from the DOH and PGZ
by debiting and crediting Drugs and Medicines for Distribution and
Income from Grants and Donations by ₱2,411,976.80, and correspondingly
record issued items therefrom and effect necessary journal entry thereof
(iii) maintain complete inventory records, in addition to those previously
maintained, such as the SPLC; and (iv) provide sufficient documents to
support the adjustments of balances; and
b. Acting MT to regularly (i) prepare and submit in addition to those
previously prepared, the RSPI, to the MAO for adjustments in the books
of consumed/issued inventories; (ii) support the issuance of semi-
expendable property to end-users with ICS to establish accountability over
the items; and (iii) maintain complete inventory records such as stock
cards, SPC and RegSPI;
c. The Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count on all inventories,
including the semi-expendable properties; and prepare and submit the
corresponding RPCIs and RPCSPs thereon to the AT not later than July
31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters,
respectively; and
d. The MHO to submit copies of Property Transfer Reports from agencies
giving medicines and other medical supplies to account properly donations
and recognize and present fairly inventories, expenses and income
accounts.

5.42 We recommended and Management agreed that the LCE to ensure
the implementation of the following corrective actions:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count on all PPEs, and prepare
and submit the corresponding RPCPPE thereon to the AT not later than
January 31 of each year;
b. MA and Acting MT to (i) consider undertaking the one-time cleansing of PPE
account balances to improve the reliability of the PPE accounts in both
accounting records and property records, thereby facilitating more effective
reconciliation between the two offices; and (ii) ensure that the PPE records are
prepared and updated in a timely manner, including PCs and PPELCs to
ensure proper control, monitoring, and accountability over the agency’s
property and equipment; and; and;
c. MA to take up the necessary adjusting entries to (i) derecognize of items of
PPE that are semi-expendable properties already issued to end-users; (ii)
recognize ongoing infrastructure projects in the CIP account as of year-end;
and (iii) capitalize borrowing costs attributable to the on-going Construction
of New Municipal Cemetery.

6.29 We recommended and Management agreed to take the following actions to
address the identified deficiencies:
a. Direct the MA to coordinate with the MHRMO to verify the accuracy of leave
credits earned by employees and prepare the necessary JEV as the initial setup
of the accounting entries to properly recognize the monetary value of the total
earned leave credits of officials and employees, as illustrated below:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Prior Period Adjustment ₱14,564,814.80
Leave Benefits Payable – Current ₱6,482,711.28
Leave Benefits Payable – Non-Current 8,082,103.52
b. Instruct the MHRMO to prioritize the review, updating, reconciliation, and
correction of leave card balances identified in the audit, and double-check the
computation of the leave credits to enhance the accuracy and integrity of
employee leave data;
c. Enjoin the MHRMO and the Department Heads to conduct regular
reconciliations to improve the process of maintaining employee leave cards
and to strengthen internal controls, thereby preventing future discrepancies
and ensuring accuracy of leave records; and
d. Require the Municipal Budget Officer to set aside additional reserve funds for
the accumulated compensated leave credits as of year-end to ensure adequate
financial provisions and avoid potential budgetary shortfalls in the future.

7.28 We recommended and Management agreed to the (a) OIC-MPDC and the
OIC-ME properly plan and closely monitor the implementation of the development
projects to prevent the incurrence of delay; (b) together with the LDC, to prepare
CLUP, CDP, and LDIP as required under Sections 106 and 109 of the LGC, and the
Guidebook issued by DILG, respectively; (c) Bids and Awards Committee prepare
and submit Procurement Monitoring Report to the GPPB as required by the 2016
RIRR of R.A. No. 9184; and (d) OIC-ME to coordinate with the contractors to ensure
regular updating of the project status to comply with the guidelines on information
and publicity on PPAs.

8.26 We recommended that Management require the LDRRM Officer to (i) closely
monitor and ensure the implementation of the LDRRM planned PPAs and reassess
the LDRRMFIP, at least quarterly, to fully optimize fund utilization; (ii) ensure full
compliance with the prescribed minimum standards for disaster preparedness set by
the DILG and LGA, as adopted by the NDRRMC; and (iii) secure a fidelity bond as
the AO for government-owned DRRM properties.

9.16 We recommended, and Management agreed to the LCE:
a) MA to (i) trace the records/documents relative to the unaccounted balance
of Due to NGAs account amounting to ₱2,324,252.75, reconcile the same,
and return any unutilized funds for completed/cancelled projects to the
source agencies; and
b) ensure that all funds received from SAs are (i) supported with a duly signed
MOA/TA to clearly define the specific guidelines and procedures in the
implementation of individual projects, including the responsibilities of the
Municipality; and (ii) timely implemented for the benefit of the intended
beneficiaries.

10.42 We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) instruct the MTO to undertake the following courses of action:
(i) prepare a stratified schedule of presumptive income levels for every
tax period to approximate the gross sales or receipt of each business
classification, and submit a representation to the Sangguniang
Bayan to establish or institutionalize its use;
(ii) regularly examine the books of accounts of business establishments
to determine the accuracy of declared gross sales or receipts used in
the computing business taxes paid, pursuant to Section 171 of RA
No. 7160;
(iii) coordinate with the ME to identify contractors that have
bunkhouses or other structures which may be considered as project
office, as a basis for imposing business tax;
(iv) collect business taxes from outside contractors engaged in business
within the Municipality, in accordance with the situs of taxation;
b) require the Palauig Tourism Council and Municipal Tourism Office, in
coordination with the BPLO, to strictly implement the provisions of the
Tourism Code, particularly Article XI on business permit, licensing, and
accreditation, to ensure that all facilities and practices meet the highest
standards for the benefit and safety of all tourists;
c) provide a written explanation or justification, along with supporting
documents, clearly stating the Municipality’s official position regarding:
(1) the operations or business transactions involving the rental of cottages
and the collection of parking fees; (2) the assignment of 2 casuals and 17
JO personnel to the Bagsit River area; and (3) media representations
indicating that the collection of cottage fees is regulated by the
Municipality. These matters have collectively contributed to the public
perception that the Municipality is the operator and, therefore, the entity
collecting the said fees.

11.11 We commended the Management for its substantial compliance with the
established headcount requirements for JO and COS personnel, as well as for
maintaining complete supporting documents for salary and wage claims. However,
we recommended and Management agreed to (i) instruct the Municipal HRMO to
submit the required locator slips/travel orders and leave applications, if any, to verify
the employees’ absence during headcount and ensure proper accountability; and (ii)
while awaiting a reply from the CSC Regional Office, strictly adhere to the CSC Rules
on Designation, particularly on the prohibition against designating casual employees
as OIC (Department Head), as mandated by Section 77 of R.A. No. 7160.

12.16 We recommended and Management agreed to GFPS (i) continue promoting
and ensuring gender mainstreaming in local policy-making, planning, programming,
budgeting, implementation, monitoring, and evaluation, as mandated by PCW,
DILG, DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; (ii) instruct the LGU GFPS to monitor
the implementation of GAD PPAs to ensure that the same are implemented as
planned to attain GAD’s goals and objectives; (iii) issue an appropriate/complete
policy directive, copy furnished to the DILG Office which shall mandate the LGU
GFPS to: a) provide technical assistance in the implementation of the GPB; b)
monitor its implementation; c) ensure the preparation and submission of GAD AR;
and d) consolidate reports on the implementation of the GPB; (iv) instruct the GFP
to submit the documentary requirements supporting the GAD AR; and (v) create a
centralized GAD Database that would consolidate the information gathered and
compiled by all offices to include gender statistics and other relevant sex-
disaggregated data as a basis in GAD planning, programming, and policy-making.

13.13 We recommended and Management agreed to the LSB (a) ensure that the
dates of transmittal of copy of SEF Budget to the concerned departments are clearly
indicated in the receiving copy on file; (b) submit the quarterly and annual reports
using the SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B) on SEF utilization, as
prepared by the MA, to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels, copy
furnished the local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and
DILG; and (c) ensure that the Quarterly Utilization Report was regularly posted in
three conspicuous public places in the Municipality.

14.7 We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations and Management agreed
to advise the MA to (a) consistently comply with BIR rules and regulations on the
withholding and remittance of taxes on compensation, purchases, and contracts, and
(b) reconcile the GL/SL balances prior to remittances to ensure the accurate and
complete remittance of all taxes withheld during the period.

15.8 We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and Management agreed
to direct the MA to (a) ensure that all withheld premium contributions and loan
amortizations are fully and promptly remitted; (b) conduct monthly reconciliation of
contributions and loans withheld and remittances with the accounting records and
effect necessary adjustments for any discrepancies noted; and (c) reconcile and trace
the balance of ₱28,726.04 for proper disposition.

16.8 We commended the Management for the prompt remittance of PhilHealth
premium contributions, demonstrating compliance with R.A. No. 10606. However, to
ensure the accuracy and completeness of remittances, we recommended and
Management agreed to instruct the MA to reconcile the GL balances prior to each
remittance.

17.8 We commended the Management for the prompt remittance of Pag-IBIG
premium contributions, demonstrating compliance with R.A. No. 9679. However, we
recommended and Management agreed to instruct the MA to reconcile the GL
balances prior to remittances to ensure the accurate and complete remittance of all
Pag-IBIG contributions during the period.

18.10 We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the LCE require all
persons responsible to settle immediately the audit suspensions and disallowances of
₱94,022,139.18.

We recommended that the LCE: a) enjoin the MA to (i) ensure that all government projects recognized/capitalized under PPE accounts met Capitalized. the conditions of a PPE as defined under IPSAS 17; (ii) ensure that all procured PPE are recorded properly recognized to appropriate PPE accounts and depreciation expense accounts therefor are recognized; (iii) update the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) for all property accounts to facilitate the reconciliation of property records between MAO and Municipal Treasury Office (MTO); and (iv) take up the necessary adjusting entries to correct the PPE accounts; b) direct the Assistant MT and/or Property Custodian to (i) maintain complete and updated property cards per category of PPE; (ii) continue to reconcile the property records with the MAO, particularly the unserviceable properties, to facilitate the disposal of items; (iii) register and insure all the motor vehicles under the name of the Municipality in the Land Transportation Office and Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), respectively; c) create an Inventory Committee who shall (i) conduct complete physical count of PPE; and (ii) prepare/update the corresponding RPCPPE and IIRUP (with appropriate remarks on the condition and whereabouts of the PPE; and d) instruct the concerned department heads to review the requirements and follow COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of PPE in order to have a verifiable data on the existence, condition and accountability of the PPEs.
observations.managementAction: All government projects were recognized/ capitalized. Strictly all acquired PPE accounts and corresponding depreciation expense accounts were recognized. PPELC were not completely updated and maintained. Property cards were not maintained. The Inventory Committee created under Executive Order No. 18 dated June 3, 2024. The Inventory Committee was not able to conduct complete physical count of PPE. JEVs were prepared. JEV Nos. 73-76 dated 8/31/24. JEV Nos. 71 and 72 dated 8/31/2024.
observations.reason: PPE records are being updated; physical count of PPE not yet completed; PPELC and property cards not fully reconciled with MAO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that each AO prepare the monthly RAAF and the Assistant MT prepare the monthly CRAAF and the submit the said monthly reports not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month to the Office of the Auditor, and the Assistant MT strictly monitor the receipts and issuances of AF and ensure that all issued AF were duly accounted and corresponding collections were fully remitted.
observations.managementAction: Monthly RAAF and CRAAF were submitted to the Audit Team (AT).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management (a) stop the practice of (i) granting additional CAs to AO despite non-liquidation or non-settlement of her previous CAs; and (ii) transferring CAs from one AO to another personnel; also, (iii) distribution or payment to recipients should be undertaken only by the MTO; (b) review/re-assess cash bond accountability of AOs and increase the bond coverage, if necessary, or designate additional AOs, if warranted; (c) ensure that (i) CAs do not exceed the maximum accountability of AO; and are completely documented; and (d) issue demand letters to the concerned municipal officials/employees for their unliquidated CAs as at December 31, 2023, and cause the withholding of their salaries upon default; otherwise, institute appropriate administrative and legal actions against AOs who continually disregard the notices/ letters sent to them.
observations.managementAction: Increased the bond coverage. CAs granted were disbursed by designated SDOs. CAs as of December 31, 2023, were already liquidated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) include in the preparation of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP)/Annual Investment Plan (AIP) the list of PPAs to be undertaken out of the unexpended balance of the prior year LDRRMF to rationalize the use of the STF; (ii) strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and objective of the fund and the desired benefits; (iii) ensure that at least 5% of the total revenues from regular sources are appropriated to the LDRRMF; and (iv) submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF within the reglementary period and the Revised/Updated LDRRMFIP to COA Office for timely review of the report.
observations.managementAction: Included in the preparation of the AIP is the list of PPAs to be undertaken out of the unexpended balance of the prior year LDRRMF to rationalize the use of the STF. At least 5% of the revenues from regular sources are appropriated to the LDRRMF. Not timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs was observed. Report on LDRRM Fund Utilization was submitted to COA Office but not strictly within the reglementary period.
observations.reason: Not all LDRRM-related PPAs were implemented timely. Report on LDRRM Fund Utilization was not submitted within the reglementary period to the COA Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the MA to trace the records/documents relative to the unaccounted balance of Due to NGAs account amounting to ₱3,482,884.67 reconcile the same and return any unutilized funds for completed/canceled projects to the source agencies. Likewise prioritize implementation of the Livelihood Program amounting to ₱500,000.00; otherwise return the same to respective SAs, immediately.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The fund transfer for the Livelihood Program amounting to ₱500,000.00 was already liquidated. The fund transfer for the Livelihood Program has already been liquidated on September 17, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the GFPS to: a) monitor the implementation of organization-focused GAD PPAs to ensure that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals and objectives; b) attach the documents required under Paragraph 5 of Section 4.0 (C.8) of JMC 2013-01 to facilitate the monitoring and evaluation of the GAD PPAs implemented during the year; c) observe the GAD AR deadline for submission of GAD AR to the AT; d) create a centralized GAD Database that would consolidate the information gathered and compiled by all offices to include gender statistics and other relevant sex-disaggregated data as a basis in GAD planning, programming, and policy-making; e) administer the HGDG test whenever it uses attribution in the budgeting phase and AR preparation; and f) seek help from experts and include HGDG training in the budget to ensure the correctness of the GAD issues identified and the responsiveness of the corresponding GAD PPAs.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The GFPS continued to promote and ensure gender mainstreaming in local policy-making, planning, programming, budgeting, implementation, monitoring, and evaluation. The GAD AR was timely submitted to the AT. GAD database was not yet consolidated. HGDG test was not administered. Training on HGDG was not conducted.
observations.reason: GAD database not yet consolidated; HGDG test not administered; some required supporting documents not attached to AR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE advise the MA to (a) continue to comply with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of taxes on compensation and payments of purchases and contracts; and (b) exert extended efforts to verify and reconcile the unremitted balance of Due to BIR account amounting to ₱56,925.50 and immediately effect the necessary adjustments and/or remittance to BIR thereafter.
observations.managementAction: Continuously complied on monthly remittances of withheld contributions. The Due to BIR account was reconciled and the necessary adjustments were effected.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Accounting Office (a) ensure that all withheld contributions and loans withheld during the period will be remitted in full; (b) conduct monthly reconciliation of contributions and loans withheld and remittances with the accounting records and effect necessary adjustments for any discrepancies noted; and (c) trace the unremitted balance of ₱355,912.84 with accounting records, payrolls, and remittances and remit the same immediately. Moreover, draw the adjusting entry to correct the non-recording of employer’s share for the TLB of the former employees.
observations.managementAction: Continuously remitted Employees Contributions. JEVs were prepared. JEV Nos. 44-45 dated 6/30/24 and JEV Nos. 77-78 dated 8/31/24.
observations.reason: Reconciliation is ongoing; not all reconciling items cleared yet.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA to (a) ensure that all premium contributions withheld are remitted intact; and (b) verify, reconcile, and trace the unaccounted negative balance of ₱15,197.92 for proper disposition. Moreover, draw the adjusting entry to correct the non-recording of employer’s share for the TLB of the former employees.
observations.managementAction: Implemented Employees Contributions were remitted. JEV Nos. 44-78 dated 6/30/24 and 8/31/24 respectively were prepared.
observations.reason: Reconciled the Due to PhilHealth account; any remaining minor variances being traced and adjusted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to consider the following measures:
a) instruct the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) to derecognize in the books the cost of supplies and materials which were already consumed and/or distributed to end-users amounting to ₱4,279,982.00 by debiting Prior Period Adjustment account and crediting Inventory accounts; and
b) require the Municipal Treasurer and/or Supply Officer to (i) prepare and submit the SSMI together with the original copy of the RIS to the Municipal Office and Accountant monthly for issued inventories based on actual Supplies consumption/issuance for accurate recording of expenses and assets in the accounts; and (ii) prepare the SCs for each type of supplies to record all receipts and issuances; 
c) direct the concerned department/units particularly the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to immediately submit to the Municipal Treasurer and/or Supply Officer the necessary report/list of inventory items that were already distributed/issued using the RIS for proper recording in the SSMI; and
d) constitute an Inventory Committee who shall promptly and regularly conduct the semestral physical count of all Inventories of the Municipality and prepare the corresponding RPCIs thereon; and thereafter, require the Accounting Office and Treasurer’s Office to reconcile the balances to establish accuracy and reliability of both the RPCIs and the financial statements as of year-end.
observations.managementAction:
Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) were included in June 2023 Financial Statements
For CY 2023, SSMI and RIS were submitted in the Accounting Office and reconciled with the Ledger Card and recorded corresponding expenses in the General Journal in December 2023.
The MDRRMO submitted a Certified List recorded as expense in June 2023.
observations.reason: reconstituted Inventory Committee was not able to conduct regular physical count.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant of a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible for the year to serve as the basis of recording; and the Municipal Accountant to make use of the Certified List of Taxpayers as the basis for setting up the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Credits at the beginning of the year; prepare JEVs to recognize/adjust the aforementioned transactions; and trace and record the necessary adjustments to reconcile the discrepancies between the amount of the recorded RPT and SET Receivables and the related Deferred Credits accounts.
observations.managementAction: creation of RPTAR database to be used as basis of Certified Lists of Taxpayers.
observations.reason: Municipal Accountant did not use the Certified List of Taxpayers as the basis for setting up the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Credits at the beginning of the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) continuously monitor receivables by sending demand letters on the delinquent accounts and intensify efforts on collection to increase RPT and SEF collection efficiency; and (ii) consider availing all legal and administrative remedies provided in R.A. No. 7160 either by administrative action through a levy on real property or judicial action as a last resort.
observations.managementAction: The MTO was not able to send demand letters to all delinquent taxpayers during CY 2023.
observations.reason: No actions were undertaken by the Management during the year.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Accounting Office (a) ensure that all contributions and loans withheld during the period will be remitted in full; (b) conduct monthly reconciliation of contributions and loans withheld and remittances with the accounting records and effect necessary adjustments for any discrepancies noted; and (c) trace the negative balance of ₱249,607.11 with accounting records, payrolls, and remittances; and make representation with the GSIS for the necessary reconciliation of records.
observations.managementAction: Not all contributions and loans withheld during the year were remitted. Monthly reconciliation was not made for CY 2023. Details of the balance as of year-end amounting to ₱203,668.41 remained unverified as of this writing.
observations.reason: Monthly reconciliation was not made; details of the unverified balance remained unverified as of this writing.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Accounting Office (a) ensure that all contributions and loans withheld during the period will be remitted in full; (b) conduct monthly reconciliation of contributions and loans withheld and remittances with the accounting records and effect necessary adjustments for any discrepancies noted; and (c) trace the unremitted balance of ₱243,624.97 with accounting records, payrolls, and remittances and remit the same immediately.
observations.managementAction: Not all contributions and loans withheld during the period were remitted. Monthly reconciliation was not made for CY 2023. Details of the balance as of year-end amounting to ₱355,912.84 is still for reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require all persons responsible to settle immediately the audit suspensions and disallowances of ₱94,022,139.18.
observations.managementAction: No actions were undertaken by the Management during the year.

observations.recommendation:
1.13 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct:
a. the MA and the Assistant MT to (i) conduct periodic reconciliation of their
records and take up the necessary adjusting entries in the books, as
appropriate; (ii) exert diligent efforts to obtain from the bank the required
documentation like bank debit and credit advice as bases in making the
necessary adjustments in the books; and (iii) coordinate with the depository
bank for possible correction/adjustment of the bank reconciling items
amounting to ₱295,353.76 (net); and
b. the MA to (i) make necessary adjustments to record (i.1) staled check and
unrecorded deposits totaling ₱8,022.11 by debiting CIB-LCCA and
crediting the corresponding Liabilities account, and (i.2) crediting CIB-
LCCA and debiting Other Receivables account by ₱400.00; and (ii) prepare
the BRS regularly and promptly adjust valid reconciling items, as
appropriate.

2.24 We recommended the management to:
a) instruct the MA to i) in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, record
the cost of partially completed infrastructure projects at year-end under the
CIP account and the corresponding Accounts Payable thereof; and ii) draw
the adjusting entries to correct the erroneous recording/non-recording of
completed projects to their appropriate PPE accounts and corresponding
accumulated depreciation;
b) ensure the commitment of the MEO to submit to the MAO a monitoring
report supported with SWA regarding the status/progress of the
construction of all ongoing projects monthly, especially at year-end to
facilitate the timely recording of CIP in the books.

3.33 We recommended that the LCE:
a) enjoin the MA to (i) ensure that all government projects recognized/capitalized
under PPE accounts met the conditions of a PPE as defined under IPSAS 17;
(ii) ensure that all procured PPE are properly recognized to appropriate PPE
accounts and depreciation expense therefor are recognized accordingly; (iii)
update the PPELC for all property accounts to facilitate the reconciliation of
property records between MAO and MTO; and (iv) take up the necessary
adjusting entries to correct the PPE accounts;
b) direct the Assistant MT and/or Property Custodian to (i) maintain complete
and updated property cards per category of PPE; (ii) continue to reconcile the
property records with the MAO, particularly the unserviceable properties, to
facilitate the disposal of items; and (iii) register and insure all the motor
vehicles under the name of the Municipality in the LTO and GSIS,
respectively;
c) create an Inventory Committee who shall (i) conduct complete physical count
of PPE; and (ii) prepare/update the corresponding RPCPPE and IIRUP (with
appropriate remarks on the condition and whereabouts of the PPE; and
d) instruct the concerned department heads to review the requirements and
follow COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of PPE in order
to have a verifiable data on the existence, condition and accountability of the
PPEs.

4.9 We recommended that each AO prepare the monthly RAAF and the Assistant
MT prepare the monthly CRAAF and submit the said monthly reports not later than
the 5th day of the ensuing month to the Office of the Auditor, and the Assistant MT
strictly monitor the receipts and issuances of AF and ensure that all issued AF were
duly accounted and corresponding collections were fully remitted.

5.21 We recommended that management (a) stop the practice of (i) granting
additional CAs to AO despite non-liquidation or non-settlement of her previous CAs;
and (ii) transferring CAs from one accountable officer to another personnel; also, (iii)
distribution or payment to recipients should be undertaken only by the MTO; (b)
review/re-assess cash accountability of AOs and increase the bond coverage, if
necessary, or designate additional AOs, if warranted; (c) ensure that (i) CAs granted
do not exceed the maximum accountability of AO; and (ii) CAs are drawn on properly
and completely documented transactions; and (d) issue demand letters to the
concerned municipal officials/ employees for their unliquidated CAs as at December
31, 2023, and cause the withholding of their salaries upon default; otherwise, institute
appropriate administrative and legal actions against accountable officers who
continually disregard the notices/ letters sent to them.

6.11 We recommended the management instruct the MA to abide strictly with the
prompt submission of CA DVs for CF in accordance with COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-
DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 08, 2015 to ensure a timely review of
the transactions. Likewise, maintain an SL solely for CF and place the CA voucher
with its supporting documents in a sealed envelope when submitting the same to COA
Audit Team Leader for post-audit to protect the confidentiality of the transaction.

7.62 We recommended that the LCE to:
a) instruct the MTO to undertake the following courses of action:
(i) prepare a stratified schedule of presumptive income level every
tax period to approximate the gross sales or receipt of each business
classification and submit a representation to the Sangguniang
Bayan to set up or institutionalize the use of the same;
(ii) regularly examine the books of accounts of business
establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross
sales or receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid
pursuant to Section 171 of RA No. 7160;
(iii)assign personnel at the MTO who are well-equipped with the
necessary knowledge and training in the examination of books of
accounts to enable the said Office to carry on with their mandate
efficiently;
(iv) tag all the afore-cited suppliers and contractors with
underdeclared sales or receipts and be subject to examination of
books of accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees, and
charges to be collected;
(v) require the concerned business establishments with unpaid
business taxes prior to their closure/retirement to pay their taxes
due based on the submitted Sworn Statement of Gross Sales or
Receipts;
(vi) require the concerned supplier with the closed business but
continued to operate, to apply for renewal, pay the renewal fee and
business tax, as well as other fees, penalties and charges, for the
period identified; and impose possible sanctions for non-compliance
with the RRC;
(vii) coordinate with the ME to identify the contractors who have
bunkhouses or other structures that may be considered as project
office as a basis for the imposition of business tax;
(viii) collect business taxes from outside contractors who engaged in
business within the Municipality applying the situs of taxation;
(ix) instruct the personnel-in-charge of the eBPLS to custom-fit or
adjust the system’s default setup with the provisions of its RRC to
avoid inaccurate computation of surcharges and interests; and
b) advise the designated BPLO to (i) require the submission of complete
requirements for the application or renewal of business permits as
required under Section 3A.03 (b)(1) and (2) of the RRC, prior to the
issuance of business permits; and (ii) ensure that these records form part
of their permanent files to easily verify the correctness of the assessments;
and
c) require the MT and the Designated BPLO to devise measures to
validate the declared gross sales or receipts of establishments applying for
business permits especially those who are having direct transactions with
the Municipality and its Barangays.

8.25 We recommended that the LCE direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) include in
the preparation of the MDRRMFIP/AIP the list of PPAs to be undertaken out of the
unexpended balance of the prior year LDRRMF to rationalize the use of the STF; (ii)
strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the
goals and objective of the fund and the desired benefits; (iii) ensure that at least 5%
of the total revenues from regular sources are appropriated to the LDRRMF; and (iv)
submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF within the
reglementary period and the Revised/Updated LDRRMFIP to COA Office for timely
review of the report.

9.31 We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that the LCE instruct
the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer:
a) to abide strictly by the project schedule by conducting regular monitoring of
the status of project implementation; and
b) together with the LDC and end-users to properly plan the PPAs to be included
in the 20% Local Development Plan, to ensure the full implementation of all
the priority projects targeted for the year for the benefit of the Municipality and
the constituents in general.
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the MPDC and implementing
units to:
c) together with the LDC to prepare CDP, and LDIP as required under Sections
106 and 109 of the LGC, and the Guidebook issued by DILG, respectively;
d) plan intelligently, prepare detailed engineering and PoWs, and consider the
needs of the constituents in formulating the developmental plans for the latter
to enjoy the benefits that can be derived from these PPAs; and
e) conduct prioritization of PPAs contained in the AIP so that specific dates of
implementation could be determined for the proper implementation and
monitoring of the same and avoid possible delays during the execution of
projects.
Finally, we recommended that the LCE instruct the Bid and Awards
Committee (BAC) to require all winning contractors to submit their respective CSHP
to DOLE for approval before the implementation of each infrastructure project; and
the ME to monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP of the contractors,
particularly the provision of personal protective equipment to construction workers,
to ensure its compliance with the requirements of the DOLE.

10.10 We recommended that Management instruct the MA to trace the
records/documents relative to the unaccounted balance of Due to NGAs account
amounting to ₱3,482,884.67 reconcile the same and return any unutilized funds for
completed/canceled projects to the source agencies. Likewise, prioritize
implementation of the Livelihood Program amounting to ₱500,000.00; otherwise
return the same to respective SAs, immediately.

11.34 We recommended that the LCE, through the SWM Board, designated
MENRO, undertake the following courses of action:
a) prioritize the implementation of solid waste management programs
and consider increasing the budget to align/harmonize with SWMP, once
approved by the NSWMC;
b) fast-track the revision of the SWMP through compliance with the
requirements in the annotated outline issued by the DENR-EMB III, for
endorsement and approval of the NSWMC;
c) (i) stop the practice of collecting and dumping mixed wastes in the
open/controlled dumpsite pursuant to the DENR Closure Notice to the LGU
on February 2011; (ii) collect only residual wastes; and (iii) consider availing
services of accredited landfills for the disposal of residual municipal solid
wastes;
d) on the implementation of the Comprehensive ESWM ordinance: (i)
exercise strong political will to implement the environmental laws and comply
with the proper segregation, collection, and disposal of wastes; (ii) strictly
implement the mandatory segregation of wastes at source thru conducting an
extensive information education campaign that would educate and encourage
the active participation of the constituents particularly the market vendors on
proper waste segregation; (iii) involve closely the LGU officials and business
establishments in the strict implementation of the existing environmental law
and intensify information campaign towards the “no segregation, no collection
policy” among the residents; (iv) create an inspection unit that will impose the
fines and penalties against violators of prohibited acts to improve compliance
rate and provide sufficient funding for SWM PPAs; and (v) increase the
compliance rate of constituents by educating the public through the conduct
of continuous forums and seminars; placing additional signages and billboards
in public places; and distribution of leaflets and flyers to raise awareness;
e) hire additional SWM personnel in charge of the Municipal MRF and
require the construction of MRF in every barangay or, if possible, resort to
clustering of MRF in small barangays with limited resources to comply with
the Act; and
f) hire additional garbage collectors and ensure that waste collectors are
equipped with PPEs to safeguard them from hazards of handling solid wastes;
and
g) expedite the purchase of vehicles specifically designed for the collection,
transport, and disposal of solid wastes compliant with the aforementioned
requirements.

12.28 We recommended that Management instruct the GFPS to:
a) monitor the implementation of organization-focused GAD PPAs to ensure
that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals and
objectives;
b) attach the documents required under Paragraph 5 of Section 4.0 (C.8) of
JMC 2013-01 to facilitate the monitoring and evaluation of the GAD PPAs
implemented during the year;
c) observe the deadline for submission of GAD AR to the AT;
d) create a centralized GAD Database that would consolidate the information
gathered and compiled by all offices to include gender statistics and other
relevant sex-disaggregated data as a basis in GAD planning, programming,
and policy-making;
e) administer the HGDG test whenever it uses attribution in the budgeting
phase and AR preparation; and
f) seek help from experts and include HGDG training in the budget to ensure
the correctness of the GAD issues identified and the responsiveness of the
corresponding GAD PPAs.

13.6 We recommended that the LCE advise the MA to (a) continue to comply with
the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of taxes on
compensation and payments of purchases and contracts; and (b) exert extended
efforts to verify and reconcile the unremitted balance of Due to BIR account
amounting to ₱56,925.50 and immediately effect the necessary adjustments and/or
remittance to BIR thereafter.

14.8 We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Accounting Office
(a) ensure that all contributions and loans withheld during the period will be remitted
in full; (b) conduct monthly reconciliation of contributions and loans withheld and
remittances with the accounting records and effect necessary adjustments for any
discrepancies noted; and (c) trace the unremitted balance of ₱355,912.84 with
accounting records, payrolls, and remittances and remit the same immediately.
Moreover, draw the adjusting entry to correct the non-recording of
employer’s share for the TLB of the former employees.

15.7 We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Accounting Office (a)
ensure that all contributions and loans withheld during the period will be remitted in
full; (b) conduct monthly reconciliation of contributions and loans withheld and
remittances with the accounting records and effect necessary adjustments for any
discrepancies noted; and (c) trace the unverified balance of ₱203,668.41 with
accounting records, payrolls, and remittances; and make representation with the
GSIS for the necessary reconciliation of records.
Moreover, draw the adjusting entry to correct the non-recording of
employer’s share for the TLB of the former employees.

16.5 We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA to (a) ensure that all premium
contributions withheld are remitted intact; and (b) verify, reconcile, and trace the
unaccounted negative balance of ₱15,197.92 for proper disposition.
Moreover, draw the adjusting entry to correct the non-recording of
employer’s share for the TLB of the former employees.

17.10 We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require all persons responsible to settle immediately the audit suspensions and
disallowances of ₱94,022,139.18.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to capitalize the borrowing costs incurred for the two projects financed out of loans; recognize accrued interest expenses on loans; and adjust the Loans Payable account based on billing statements/SIPBs of banking institutions, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: JEVs were included on the June 2023 Financial Statements

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) trace the records/documents relative to the balances in the unaccounted balance of Due to NGAs account amounting to ₱3,342,489.40, reconcile the same, and return any unutilized funds for completed/cancelled projects to the source agencies; and (ii) henceforth, account the revenues from NGAs in the TF books by recognizing Subsidy from National Government after the fulfillment of the conditions or purpose stipulated in the agreement and recognize the corresponding expenditures under the appropriate under the Assets/Expenses accounts.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2023, revenues from NGAs in the TF books were properly accounted and the corresponding expenditures were recognized under the appropriate Assets/Expenses accounts.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management instruct the OIC-MPDC and the Municipal Engineer together with the Local Development Council and end-users to properly plan the PPAs to be included in the 20% Local Development Plan, and/or immediately act on the realignment of funds, if needed, so that the project will be implemented during the year ; and see to it that there are no duplication of PPAs chargeable against different funds of the Agency, to ensure the full implementation of all the priority projects targeted for the year for the benefit of the Municipality and the constituents in general.
observations.managementAction: Out of the 23 PPAs in the current appropriation, only 2 PPAs costing ₱22,061,393.51 were completed in CY 2023. Meanwhile, out of 5 PPAs from the continuing appropriations, only 1 PPA amounting to ₱19,331,929.62 has been implemented and completed while 25 PPAs with total appropriations of ₱25,879,408.14 were not implemented at all as of year-end.
observations.reason: Management prioritized the implementation of PPAs that are more urgent in nature and relevant; however, many PPAs included in the AIP were not implementation-ready (no DED/PoWs) and some were deferred for bidding or reprogramming.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRMC to (i) henceforth ensure that the LDRRMF is optimally utilized particularly on proper planning for the inclusion/integration of activities to be funded from the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF into the LDRRMFIP based on an extensive evaluation of the vulnerability of the Municipality to natural or man-made disaster risks to rationalize the use of the STF; and (ii) formulate of efficient relief distribution mechanism as incorporated in the DRRM plans.
observations.managementAction: The PPAs funded under the STF were not included in the LDRRMIP. Relief distribution sheets with actual quantity received per recipient were incorporated in the DRRM plans.
observations.reason: The PPAs funded under the STF were not included in the LDRRMIP at the beginning of the year which resulted in slow utilization; some PPAs require additional funding and planning for implementation in succeeding year.

observations.managementAction:
Actual expenditures reported in the GAD-AR disclosed minimal or no expenditures incurred for the implementation of several PPAs because the activities were either conducted without incurring expenditures or lack of available trainers for trainings and workshops.
Data and GAD-related information were again requested from concerned offices and departments and were not consolidated for data processing, analysis, and updating.
GAD key personnel attended online training on gender and development.
observations.reason: Reported expenditures in GAD-AR were minimal or none due to activities conducted without incurring expenditures or lack of available trainers; data requested were not consolidated; Responsibility Centers not established during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to: a) instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) trace the records/documents relative to the unaccounted balance of Due to NGAs account amounting to ₱3,342,489.40, reconcile the same, and return any unutilized funds for completed/cancelled projects to the source agencies; and b) require the Fund transfers personnel concerned to (i) strictly comply with the MOAs for fund transfers from NGAs; (ii) liquidate the fund transfers within the prescribed period; (iii) implement the projects in a timely manner to avoid loss of funds for implementation of various PPAs for the constituents.
observations.managementAction: Fund transfers during the year were liquidated and implemented in a timely manner.
observations.reason: Reconciliation by the MAO was not undertaken earlier due to unavailability of records; some prior year unaccounted balances remain under reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the HRMO (i) prepare and issue policy guidelines subject to the approval of the Municipal Mayor to address the issues on the necessity and propriety of hiring casuals, COS and job orders in order to limit expenditures to programs/projects where their services are actually needed; and re-evaluate the personnel complement to fill-up the vacancies; (ii) stop the practice of hiring local elective officials in any capacity to any public office or position; and (iii) ensure that claims of salaries and wages of aforementioned personnel were supported with proper and complete documentary requirements.
observations.managementAction: No local elective officials were hired during the year. Accomplishment reports in the claims for wages of consultants were attached.
observations.reason: No actions were undertaken by the Management during the year to issue policy guidelines; however, no elective officials were hired and some documentary support were provided for consultants.

observations.recommendation:
1.5 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct
the Municipal Accountant to:
a) draw adjusting entries to correct the misstatements of the PPE accounts;
(i) transfer of completed projects to appropriate PPE accounts; (ii)
recognize depreciation expenses for completed Projects; and (iii)
reclassify semi-expendable and consumable items to Inventory/Expenses;
and
b) henceforth, ensure that (i) completed projects in the CIP accounts are
reclassified to appropriate Asset accounts; (ii) progress billings for
infrastructure projects during the year are recorded at year-end; and (iii)
small tangible items costing below ₱15,000.00 are classified to the
appropriate Inventory/Expenses accounts for accurate and fair
presentation of the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

2.4 We recommended and the LCE agreed to consider the following measures:
a) instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) draw JEVs taking up the
issued/consumed supplies and materials at year-end; and (ii) maintain
supplies ledger cards for all inventory accounts to support the recorded
balances of inventory; and
b) require the Municipal Treasury and/or Supply Officer to (i) prepare the
stock cards for the purchased supplies; (ii) conduct periodic inventory of
supplies as prescribed by the above regulation; (iii) prepare the
corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Inventories and submit
the same to the Accounting Office for reconciliation with the reported
inventory balances in the books; and (iv) prepare and submit to the
Accounting Office the SSMIs monthly for issued inventories based on
actual consumption/issuance for accurate recording of expenses and
assets accounts for the period.

3.6 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a) instruct the Budget Officer to explain why the COVID-19 HP was paid
using the appropriation intended for payment of wages of labor workers,
prior to augmentation or realignment of funds;
b) advise the HRMO to submit, if any, the required (i) order authorizing the
employees to physically report for work; and (ii) copy of the duties and
responsibilities of the recipients during the period of grant. Further,
instruct her to explain why she failed to demand the AWA adopted by
each department, monitor the reporting to office of the employees, their
submission of DTRs based on the entries in the logbook of each
department, and her laxity in checking the correctness and reliability of
the number of days the concerned personnel reported for duty during
ECQ;
c) instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) submit the lacking
DTRs/Logbook; (ii) Accomplishment Reports of the aforementioned
personnel; and (iii) to explain why some employees were able to claim
more than what was allowed per DTRs and why the number of days
reported for work as per payroll, DTRs and certifications issued by the
Municipal Mayor/Vice Mayor/Department Heads varied; and
d) advise the Immediate Supervisor of 126 employees to refund excess
claims totaling ₱958,350.00.

4.6 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a) instruct all Officials/Employees/Barangay and National Government
Agencies’ employees to refund the meal allowance claimed for lack of
legal basis;
b) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to see to it that proper identification
and authority of the payees were attached to payrolls for claims received
by other persons other than the payee; and
c) instruct the Human Resource Management Officer, the Municipal
Accountant and the Department Heads concerned to evaluate and
explain the discrepancies noted on the payment of meal allowance.

5.16 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the:
a) MSWDO to (i) perform complete validation of SAC Forms of 110
aforementioned beneficiaries to establish their qualifications and submit
to the Audit Team the result thereof; and (ii) submit written explanation
on the noted ineligible beneficiaries, if any; and
b) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MSWDO, to (i) submit
the accomplished SAC Forms, barangay certification, printed copy of
properly labeled pictures of the beneficiaries during actual receipt of the
assistance and valid IDs of five beneficiaries mentioned above; (ii) ensure
that all disbursement vouchers are properly supported with complete
documentation before making any payment.

6.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a) strictly adhere with the prescribed guidelines on the utilization of
succeeding grants and fund transfers particularly on (i) the use of the
Fund solely for its intended purpose and refrain from mismatching
actual disbursements to approved appropriation per specific
program/project/activities; (ii) creation of special account for monitoring
purposes; and (iii) comply with the reporting and posting requirements;
and
b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare the aforementioned
correcting entry by debiting Prior Period Adjustment and crediting
Subsidy from National Government in the amount of ₱692,204.60; and
(ii) submit the aforementioned deficient documents to the Audit Team.

7.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a) direct the MDRRMC to carefully plan and execute LDRRM-related
PPAs to achieved the goals and objective of the fund and the desired
benefits, including policy/guidelines in the distribution of Emergency
Shelter Assistance and other forms of assistance to be charged under the
Fund; and
b) instruct the Municipal Accountant and BAC to (i) submit the
aforementioned required documents immediately to the Office of the
Auditor for proper/further evaluation of the transactions; and (ii)
comply with the required reportorial requirements of GPPB Circular
2020-001 for information purposes and instruct the MDRRM Officer
and Municipal accountant to ensure timely preparation of the Report on
Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the
end of each month and submit to the Office of the Auditor within the
aforesaid reglementary period.

8.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a) instruct the OIC-MPDC and the OIC-Municipal Engineer to conduct
regular monitoring of the status of project implementation to ensure
timely execution of PPAs;
b) direct the OIC-MPDC, the OIC-Municipal Engineer and concerned
officials/end-users to properly plan the PPAs to be included in the 20%
Local Development Plan and consider other projects relevant to the
attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental
management outcomes as identified under DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1;
and
c) require each Punong Barangay to submit proposals for projects needed
by the Barangay for the enjoyment and benefit of their constituents.

9.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the GFPS to:
a) continuously comply with the GAD mechanisms and processes to sustain
the LGU’s critical consciousness in supporting gender and development,
women’s empowerment and responding to gender issues; and
b) ensure complete and timely implementation of GAD PPAs so that desired
results and benefits for the intended recipients/beneficiaries are fully
achieved.

10.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the:
a) OIC-Municipal Engineer to (i) validate substantially the reasons for the
requests of suspension of works in accordance with the requirements set
forth under DPWH DO No. 156, s. 2015; and (ii) henceforth, indicate the
number of days of suspension before recommending the approval of the
Suspension Orders; and
b) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the OIC-Municipal
Engineer, to (i) submit the certified copy of Project Logbook and
Statement of Time Elapsed on the two completed infrastructure projects;
(ii) direct the contractor to submit the required supporting documents to
the request for suspension of work in the construction of Public Market
and New Municipal Hall, otherwise, impose liquidated damages
equivalent to number of days delayed not considering the unsupported
suspension orders; and (iii) ensure that all disbursement vouchers are
properly supported with complete documentation before making any
payment.

11.6 We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
a) instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), together with its TWG
and Secretariat to (i) submit copies of the contracts and all supporting
documents within five working days from execution to the Audit Team;
and (ii) abide the required posting of Notice of Award of Contract within
three days after its issuance to inform the public of the result of the
bidding;
b) direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the BAC and the
OIC-Municipal Engineer for the completion and submission of the
documentary requirements noted in audit and ensure that only
disbursement vouchers with complete documentation are processed and
presented for payment; and (ii) adhere to the withholding of ten percent
(10%) retention money on progress payments to contractors to cover
possible uncorrected discovered defects and third-party liabilities; and
c) instruct the OIC-Municipal Engineer to (i) coordinate with the
contractors to comply with the required posting of information and
publicity on PPAs of government agencies; and (ii) inform the Audit
Team within ten (10) days after the award of the project or before the
start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboards and/or public
notices are already posted at the project site.

12.12 We appreciated the effort of management in the prompt remittance of the
current contributions and loan amortizations due the GSIS and for complying
strictly with the provisions of Republic Act No. 8291. Notwithstanding, we
recommended that the Accounting Office (a) ensure that all contributions and loans
withheld during the period will be remitted in full; and (b) perform the necessary
reconciliation with the GSIS to trace the negative balance of ₱47,400.89 for proper
disposition.

13.10 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Accounting staff in
charge of Pag-IBIG remittances (a) observe full remittance of fund contributions
and loan payments deducted from employees’ compensation to avoid imposition of
penalties and surcharges for late and incomplete remittances; and (b) trace and
remit in full the unremitted balances of ₱76,571.21.

14.10 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant
to (a) continue to comply with the required amounts of deduction and period of
remittance of the PhilHealth premium contributions; and (b) exert more effort to
trace the previous year’s negative balance of ₱8,020.74 of Due to PhilHealth
account.

15.11 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant
to (a) continually abide with the BIR regulation on the deadline of remittance of
taxes withheld from employees as well as from suppliers/contractors; (b) direct the
personnel in-charge to determine the exact amount to be remitted to arrive at an
accurate balance of the Due to BIR at year-end; (c) determine the causes of over or
under remittance that eventually distorted the account balance and coordinate with
the BIR as regards possible offsetting of the excessive remittances against the
amounts due in the succeeding remittance period; and (d) draw adjusting entry to
adjust the Due to BIR account.

16.8 We recommended and the LCE agreed to require all persons responsible to
settle immediately the audit suspensions and disallowances of ₱94,022,139.18.
